Deep tendon reflexes are 2+ and symmetric. Sensation is intact to light touch and pinprick. Laboratory tests reveal normal blood counts and metabolic panel, but inflammatory markers are slightly elevated. Electromyography and nerve conduction studies are ordered. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 36-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain, primarily affecting her wrists and fingers. She describes morning stiffness lasting approximately 1 hour which improves as the day progresses. She has also noticed some swelling and warmth over the affected joints. She denies any fever, weight loss, or skin rashes. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She does not smoke and consumes alcohol occasionally. On physical examination, there is symmetrical swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) levels. Rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (ACPAs) are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 30-year-old woman comes to her primary care physician with a 3-month history of persistent cervical lymphadenopathy, night sweats, and unintentional weight loss of 5 kg (11 lb). She has also experienced fatigue and occasional joint pain, particularly involving the small joints of her hands. Her past medical history includes seasonal allergies and she takes loratadine as needed. She denies any recent travel, sick contacts, or use of illicit drugs. On examination, her temperature is 37.4°C (99.3°F), pulse is 78/min, and blood pressure is 118/76 mm Hg. Multiple non-tender, firm cervical lymph nodes are palpated bilaterally. The remainder of the physical examination is unremarkable. Laboratory studies show hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL, leukocyte count 8,500/mm³ with a normal differential, and an elevated ESR. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the evaluation of this patient?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 3-month history of progressive fatigue, generalized joint pain, and occasional fever. She has also noticed easy bruising and frequent nosebleeds over the past few weeks. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she is not on any medications. Physical examination reveals pallor, petechiae on the lower extremities, and mild hepatosplenomegaly. Laboratory tests show pancytopenia with hemoglobin 9.2 g/dL, platelet count 45,000/mm³, and white blood cell count 2,800/mm³. Reticulocyte count is low. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 25-year-old woman comes to the clinic with lower back pain that has been present for the past 8 months. She describes the pain as being most severe in the mornings and notes that it tends to improve with physical activity. She has also experienced intermittent bouts of fatigue and stiffness in her hips. She denies any recent trauma or history of injury. Her family history is significant for an aunt with rheumatoid arthritis. Her vital signs are normal. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the sacroiliac joints and limited spinal flexion. Laboratory tests show elevated C-reactive protein levels. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 47-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of fatigue and joint pain. She reports that the pain is most pronounced in her wrists, hands, and knees, and is associated with morning stiffness lasting over an hour. She has also noticed some swelling in these joints. The patient denies any recent trauma or significant illness. She has a history of hypothyroidism, managed with levothyroxine. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally.

On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints and both wrists. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL (reference range: 12.0–16.0 g/dL) with a mean corpuscular volume of 88 fL (reference range: 80–100 fL), and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 48 mm/h (reference range: 0–20 mm/h). Radiographs of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of tingling and numbness in her hands and feet. She describes intermittent burning sensations, particularly at night. The symptoms began in her toes and fingertips and have progressively worsened. She denies any recent trauma. She reports fatigue and occasional joint pains, but no morning stiffness. Her medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension. She takes metformin and lisinopril. On examination, there is reduced sensation to light touch and pinprick in a stocking-glove distribution. Her pulse is 80/min, blood pressure is 132/78 mm Hg, and she is afebrile. Laboratory studies show normal blood counts and glucose levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of persistent fatigue and weight gain over the past six months. She notes a decrease in her energy levels, even after a full night's sleep. She also reports feeling colder than usual and experiencing hair thinning. Her medical history is unremarkable except for occasional joint pain, which she attributes to her job as a graphic designer. She denies any fevers, night sweats, or significant dietary changes. Physical examination reveals dry skin and a diffusely enlarged thyroid gland. Initial laboratory tests show a low free T4 and elevated TSH. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of diffuse musculoskeletal pain, profound fatigue, and non-restorative sleep. She describes the pain as aching and burning, most prominent in her shoulders, neck, and back. She has tried over-the-counter NSAIDs without relief. She reports waking up frequently at night, feeling unrested in the morning, and experiencing frequent headaches. She denies joint swelling or redness. Her medical history is significant for anxiety and irritable bowel syndrome. She is currently not on any medications. Physical examination reveals multiple tender points over the trapezius, lateral epicondyle, and gluteal regions. Neurological examination is normal, and there are no signs of synovitis or joint effusion. Laboratory studies show normal complete blood count, normal C-reactive protein, and normal erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Antinuclear antibody and rheumatoid factor tests are negative. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 41-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of worsening fatigue, weight gain, and cold intolerance. She also reports occasional joint pain, particularly in her wrists and knees, but denies morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes. She has felt increasingly depressed and her memory has been worsening. Her medical history is significant for type 2 diabetes mellitus, for which she takes metformin. She has no family history of thyroid disorders but her mother has rheumatoid arthritis. On physical examination, her vital signs are within normal limits, but her skin appears dry and she has mild periorbital puffiness. Laboratory tests show elevated thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH), low free thyroxine (T4), and positive antithyroid peroxidase antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 36-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of persistent cough, nasal congestion, and fatigue. She reports occasional blood-tinged sputum and has lost 3 kg unintentionally. She has a past medical history of allergic rhinitis and has been treated recently with multiple courses of antibiotics and nasal corticosteroids, with minimal improvement. She denies any fever, night sweats, or recent travel. Physical examination reveals bilateral nasal mucosal edema and crackles in the lower lung fields. Laboratory tests show a creatinine level of 2.1 mg/dL (baseline 0.8 mg/dL) and urinalysis indicates hematuria and proteinuria. A chest x-ray shows multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules. What is the most likely diagnosis given this presentation?

A 58-year-old woman with a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia presents to the clinic with chronic bilateral hand and wrist pain for the past year. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and has noticed several subcutaneous nodules on her forearms. Her medications include lisinopril and atorvastatin. She denies smoking and drinks wine occasionally. Family history is significant for rheumatoid arthritis in her mother. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Subcutaneous nodules are palpable on the extensor surfaces of the forearms. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers and a positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 24-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of acute onset joint pain and swelling in her wrists, knees, and ankles. She reports these symptoms started shortly after returning from a trip to South America. She also describes a rash that appeared on her face, fever, and mild fatigue. Her temperature is 38.1°C (100.6°F), blood pressure is 110/68 mm Hg, pulse is 95/min, and respirations are 18/min. On examination, there is notable swelling and tenderness in the affected joints, but no deformities are observed. Laboratory tests show a mild leukocytosis and elevated C-reactive protein. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with fatigue, generalized joint pains, and subcutaneous nodules on her elbows and forearms. She reports that the nodules are non-tender and have been present for several months, but she has been experiencing increasing fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers. She denies any recent infections or significant weight loss. On physical examination, she has multiple firm, movable nodules on the extensor surfaces of her arms. Her temperature is 98.6°F (37°C), blood pressure is 120/78 mmHg, pulse is 76/min, respirations are 16/min, and oxygen saturation is 99% on room air. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL, an ESR of 45 mm/hr, and a CRP of 10 mg/L. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-month history of progressive shoulder pain and restricted range of motion. She has difficulty performing overhead activities and reaching behind her back. The pain is worse at night, disrupting her sleep. She denies any history of trauma or specific injury to the shoulder. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia, managed with lisinopril and atorvastatin. On examination, there is tenderness over the anterior shoulder and a reduced active range of motion, especially in abduction and external rotation. There is no swelling or redness. Neurological examination of the upper limbs is normal. X-ray of the shoulder shows no fractures or dislocations, but the joint space is narrowed. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old woman presents to the clinic with fatigue, malaise, and a recent onset of subcutaneous nodules on her elbows and forearms. She describes the nodules as firm, non-tender, and has noticed them over the past month. She also reports intermittent fevers and unintentional weight loss of 4 kg over the past two months. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia, for which she takes lisinopril and atorvastatin. On physical examination, there are multiple firm nodules located on the extensor surfaces of her elbows and forearms. No joint swelling or tenderness is noted. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor is positive, while antinuclear antibody (ANA) and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (ACPA) are negative. An ultrasound of the soft tissue nodules reveals hypoechoic lesions with increased vascularity. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 53-year-old woman presents to the physician with a 4-month history of joint pain and swelling in her hands and wrists. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and fatigue throughout the day. She has a history of hypertension managed with lisinopril and no other significant medical history. There is no family history of autoimmune diseases. On physical examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints with decreased grip strength. Her laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. Radiographs of the hands reveal periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with severe back pain that started suddenly after she bent over to pick up a book. She describes the pain as sharp and localized to the mid-back, with no radiation. She denies any recent trauma, falls, or changes in bowel or bladder habits. She reports a history of intermittent joint pain and stiffness, particularly in her hands, for the past few years, but has not sought medical evaluation for these symptoms. She is postmenopausal and has not been on hormone replacement therapy. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. Her temperature is 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure is 130/85 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respiratory rate is 14/min. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the T12 vertebra and limited range of motion due to pain. Neurological examination is normal. A plain radiograph of the thoracic spine reveals a compression fracture at T12. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.B: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the spine
options.D: Initiation of calcium and vitamin D supplementation
options.F: CT scan of the thoracic spine

question: A 42-year-old woman visits her rheumatologist with concerns of worsening joint pain and swelling over the past six months. She describes the pain as predominantly affecting her hands and knees, noting stiffness lasting over an hour each morning. On examination, there is notable swelling and tenderness in the proximal interphalangeal joints and wrists bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers, positive rheumatoid factor, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. A recent X-ray of her hands showed joint space narrowing and erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's condition?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of rash on her lower legs and blood in her urine for the past week. She has a history of occasional joint pain and fatigue but has not experienced these symptoms recently. She denies any recent travel, new medications, or changes in diet. Her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), blood pressure is 115/75 mm Hg, pulse is 78/min, and respiratory rate is 18/min. Physical examination reveals palpable purpura on her lower extremities and mild pretibial edema. Urinalysis confirms hematuria and mild proteinuria. Laboratory tests show normal complete blood count and renal function, but elevated serum IgA levels. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 58-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to worsening pain and deformity in her hands over the past several years. She reports that her fingers are becoming increasingly stiff and twisted, especially in the mornings, which makes daily tasks difficult. She also experiences occasional fatigue and low-grade fevers. She has a history of anemia of chronic disease. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with notable ulnar deviation. Laboratory studies show a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Radiographs of the hands demonstrate joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 35-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of progressively worsening weakness in her legs over the past 3 months. She reports occasional difficulty climbing stairs and getting up from a seated position. Her medical history is significant for recurrent episodes of joint pain and morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which she attributed to overwork and stress. She denies numbness, tingling, or loss of sensation. On physical examination, there is 4/5 muscle strength in the proximal lower extremities bilaterally, mild synovitis of both wrists, and tenderness at the metacarpophalangeal joints. Laboratory tests show elevated creatine kinase levels and positive antinuclear antibody titers. Electromyography indicates myopathic changes. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 27-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of swollen lymph nodes in her neck and underarms. She reports losing 5 kg (11 lbs) unintentionally during this period and experiencing night sweats. Additionally, she mentions a persistent low-grade fever and mild fatigue. She denies any recent travel, sick contacts, or new medications. On examination, she appears pale and has multiple tender cervical and axillary lymph nodes. There is no skin rash or joint swelling. Laboratory tests show:
Hemoglobin: 10.5 g/dL
Leukocyte count: 9,500/mm3
Platelet count: 210,000/mm3
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate: 45 mm/h
Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH): 320 U/L
Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?

options.D: Order CT scan of the chest and abdomen

question: A 56-year-old woman presents to the clinic with chronic pain and swelling in both hands and wrists that has been worsening over the past 6 months. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and describes difficulty performing tasks such as opening jars. She denies any recent trauma or fever. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Subcutaneous nodules are palpated over the extensor surfaces of her elbows. Laboratory tests show elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are present. Hand radiographs reveal joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents with a 6-month history of progressive deformity in her hands and feet. She reports that her fingers are stiff and painful, especially in the morning, lasting for more than an hour. She also mentions experiencing fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers. She has a history of hypertension, managed with lisinopril. Physical examination reveals joint swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, along with ulnar deviation of the fingers. Laboratory tests show elevated rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-rays of the hands demonstrate erosions at the joint margins. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents with a 3-month history of fatigue, joint pain, and pale skin. She mentions increased difficulty in performing her daily activities due to weakness and persistent pain in her wrists and knees. She denies any recent infections or unusual stress. Her menstrual periods have become irregular. On examination, she appears pale and lethargic, with mild swelling and tenderness in her wrist joints. A complete blood count reveals pancytopenia. Her serum creatinine, liver function tests, and electrolytes are within normal limits. An antinuclear antibody test is positive at a low titer. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the evaluation of this patient?

options.B: Measurement of serum vitamin B12 levels

question: A 30-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of fatigue, night sweats, and unintentional weight loss of 10 pounds. She reports a low-grade fever every evening and denies any recent infections or travel. Physical examination reveals cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy, as well as mild splenomegaly. Laboratory tests show elevated ESR and CRP. A lymph node biopsy is performed, showing atypical lymphoid cells. She has a history of occasional joint pain but no morning stiffness or joint swelling. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman comes to the clinic complaining of joint pain and swelling for the past 4 months. She reports that the pain is worse in the morning and improves as the day progresses. She also notes fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers. There is no history of trauma or significant illness. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she does not take any medications. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with decreased grip strength. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 68-year-old woman presents to the clinic with acute onset of severe back pain that began after she lifted a heavy box at home. She describes the pain as sharp and localized to the mid-thoracic region. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, and she has been postmenopausal for 18 years. She reports chronic joint pain and stiffness in her hands and knees, particularly in the mornings, for the past two years, but has not sought medical attention for these symptoms before. She denies any neurological symptoms such as numbness or tingling in her legs. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the T8-T10 vertebrae. An X-ray of the thoracic spine shows a compression fracture at T9. Her laboratory results show normal calcium and vitamin D levels, but a slightly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). What is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

options.D: Testing for rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies

question: A 45-year-old woman presents to the neurology clinic with a 6-month history of intermittent neck pain accompanied by tingling and numbness in her right arm and hand. She works as an office assistant and spends long hours typing on a computer. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or weight loss. Physical examination reveals decreased grip strength in the right hand and diminished sensation over the right C6 dermatome. Additionally, there is mild tenderness over the cervical paraspinal muscles. An MRI of the cervical spine shows a herniated disc at the C5-C6 level with mild spinal cord compression. What is the most appropriate next step in management for this patient?

options.C: Refer to a pain management specialist

question: A 24-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 2-week history of painful, red nodules on her shins and joint pain in her knees and ankles. She reports feeling fatigued and has noticed some weight loss. She denies any recent travel or exposure to new medications. Her menstrual cycles are regular, and she has no significant past medical history. She is not on any medications. Physical examination reveals multiple tender, erythematous nodules on the anterior aspect of both lower legs. Her knees and ankles are swollen and tender, but there is no significant stiffness in the morning. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 46-year-old woman comes to your office with complaints of fatigue, joint stiffness, and unexplained weight gain over the past few months. She notices that her face, especially around her eyes, seems more swollen in the mornings. She denies changes in her diet, recent travel, or new medications. Her medical history is notable for well-controlled hypothyroidism on levothyroxine. On physical examination, there is periorbital edema and her hands show slight swelling with decreased range of motion in the morning. Her vital signs are normal. Laboratory tests reveal normal thyroid function tests but decreased serum albumin levels. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 2-week history of diffuse body aches and fatigue following recovery from an upper respiratory tract infection. She reports generalized muscle soreness and joint stiffness, particularly in the mornings, but no swelling or redness of the joints. She has also experienced intermittent low-grade fevers. Her past medical history is notable for anxiety, for which she takes sertraline. She denies any recent travel or new medications. Physical examination shows tenderness to palpation over the trapezius and lumbar areas, with normal range of motion in all joints. There is no synovitis or rash. Laboratory tests reveal a normal complete blood count and basic metabolic panel, but an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 42 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for her symptoms?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 6-month history of chronic pain and morning stiffness affecting her hands and wrists. She describes the stiffness as lasting more than an hour upon waking and gradually improving throughout the day. On physical exam, there is swelling and tenderness noted in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with several subcutaneous nodules palpated over the extensor surfaces of the forearms. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, treated with levothyroxine. Laboratory studies reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), positive rheumatoid factor (RF), and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 46-year-old woman presents to the clinic with concerns about unexplained weight gain and facial puffiness over the last three months. She also reports stiffness in her fingers and knees, which is most pronounced in the morning and eases as the day progresses. She denies significant changes in her diet or exercise routine. Her past medical history includes episodic migraines and seasonal allergies. Her current medications include sumatriptan and loratadine. On physical examination, periorbital edema is noted, and there is mild tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Blood pressure is 138/84 mm Hg, and laboratory results reveal a serum creatinine of 1.2 mg/dL and a urine dipstick positive for protein. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the neurology clinic with progressive difficulty walking over the past 3 months. She describes weakness in her legs that has been worsening and now struggles with climbing stairs and rising from a seated position. She also notes fatigue and occasional blurry vision that comes and goes. Her past medical history is significant for eczema and seasonal allergies. She has no family history of neurological disorders. On examination, she has reduced strength in the proximal muscles of her lower extremities, hyperreflexia, and a positive Babinski sign bilaterally. Sensation is intact, and cranial nerves are unremarkable. Laboratory work reveals normal thyroid function, and an MRI of the brain is ordered. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 65-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 2-week history of severe back pain following a minor fall at home. She describes the pain as sharp and localized to her lower thoracic spine. She denies any trauma or heavy lifting. Her medical history includes mild osteopenia, diagnosed 5 years ago, and hypertension, which is well controlled on lisinopril. She does not take any medications for her bone health. Her mother had osteoporosis. On physical examination, her height is measured to be 2 inches shorter than a year ago. There is tenderness over the T10 vertebra with no neurological deficits. Her vital signs are within normal limits. A plain X-ray of the thoracic spine shows a compression fracture at T10. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in managing this patient?

options.C: Order MRI of the thoracic spine
options.F: Prescribe calcium and vitamin D supplementation

question:
A 34-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to joint pain and swelling over the past 3 months. She notes that the pain is bilateral, affecting her wrists, fingers, and knees, and tends to be worse in the morning, improving as the day progresses. She also reports feeling fatigued and has lost 5 pounds unintentionally over the past few months. She has no significant medical history and takes no medications regularly. Her temperature is 37.4°C (99.3°F), blood pressure is 125/80 mm Hg, pulse is 78/min, and respiratory rate is 14/min. Physical examination reveals swelling, tenderness, and limited range of motion in both wrists and several proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL
Leukocyte count 9,800
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 45 mm/hr
C-reactive protein 18 mg/L
The rheumatoid factor test is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of persistent fatigue and new-onset hypertension. She reports increased swelling in her ankles and occasional headaches over the past three weeks. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She denies alcohol or tobacco use. On physical examination, her blood pressure is 165/95 mm Hg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated serum creatinine level and mild proteinuria. The clinician suspects an underlying condition contributing to her symptoms. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

options.A: Initiate antihypertensive therapy with an ACE inhibitor
options.B: Evaluate for rheumatoid arthritis with rheumatoid factor testing
options.C: Perform renal ultrasound to assess for renal artery stenosis
options.E: Initiate corticosteroid therapy for suspected lupus nephritis
options.F: Order a 24-hour urine collection for protein quantification
options.G: Conduct an ANA test for systemic lupus erythematosus

question: A 29-year-old female presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of right knee pain and swelling that began after a long hike in the mountains. She reports no previous knee injuries, but recalls her knee giving way once during the hike. The pain has persisted, and she describes it as sharp and exacerbated by movement. She denies fever, rash, or any systemic symptoms. Her medical history is significant for asthma, for which she uses an albuterol inhaler as needed. She does not smoke and consumes alcohol occasionally. On examination, her temperature is 98.2°F (36.8°C), blood pressure is 112/72 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 14/min. The right knee is swollen with a moderate effusion, and there is tenderness on palpation along the medial joint line, but no erythema or warmth. The McMurray test is positive. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 25-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of lower back pain. She reports that the pain is worse in the morning and improves with activity throughout the day. She also notes occasional heel pain and stiffness in her hips. She denies any recent trauma or infections. Her family history is significant for her father having psoriasis. Her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), blood pressure is 118/76 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the sacroiliac joints and reduced range of motion in the lumbar spine. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's condition?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with 3 years of progressive hand and wrist pain, which is worse in the mornings and improves throughout the day. She also reports stiffness lasting over an hour in the morning and has noticed swelling in her wrists and fingers as well as small, firm nodules under her skin near the elbows. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, well-controlled on lisinopril. She denies fevers, weight loss, or new skin rashes. Physical examination reveals swollen and tender bilateral metacarpophalangeal and wrist joints, along with subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory tests show an elevated rheumatoid factor and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Radiographs of the hands demonstrate joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 4-month history of swollen and painful joints. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour. The pain primarily affects her wrists, knees, and small joints of the hands, particularly the proximal interphalangeal joints. She also describes fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers but denies recent infections or injuries. Her past medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, for which she takes lisinopril and atorvastatin, respectively. On physical examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated ESR of 55 mm/hr (normal < 20 mm/hr) and positive rheumatoid factor. An x-ray of the hands shows juxta-articular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman visits her primary care clinic due to persistent fatigue and joint pain in her hands and wrists for the last 4 months. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour that improves throughout the day. She has noticed swelling and warmth in her wrists and several proximal interphalangeal joints. Her medical history is unremarkable except for occasional headaches. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 10.8 g/dL with a mean corpuscular volume of 89 fL, an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and a positive rheumatoid factor. Physical examination notes symmetrical swelling and tenderness in the small joints of both hands. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 29-year-old woman visits her physician complaining of painful red nodules on her shins for the past week. She also reports joint pain in her knees and ankles, along with fatigue and low-grade fever. She denies any recent respiratory symptoms or gastrointestinal disturbances. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. Physical examination reveals several tender, erythematous nodules on her lower extremities, with no rash elsewhere on her body. Her vital signs show a temperature of 100.2°F (37.9°C), blood pressure of 118/75 mmHg, pulse of 82/min, and respirations of 16/min. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and normal complete blood count. A chest radiograph shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old female comes to the clinic reporting joint pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists for the past six months. She notes that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning, lasting for several hours before improving throughout the day. Her past medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, for which she takes lisinopril and atorvastatin. On physical examination, the metacarpophalangeal joints and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands are swollen and tender. There is no evidence of deformities or synovial effusion. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibody is elevated. X-rays of her hands show soft tissue swelling around the affected joints but no erosions.

What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's condition?

A 29-year-old woman visits her dermatologist complaining of a rash on her face that becomes more pronounced after sun exposure. She also reports joint pain, particularly in her hands and knees, and fatigue that has progressively worsened over the past few months. She has noticed several painless ulcers in her mouth but denies any new medications or recent illnesses. Her medical history is insignificant, and she takes no regular medications. Physical examination reveals an erythematous malar rash sparing the nasolabial folds and tenderness in multiple small joints of the hands. Laboratory tests show a mild anemia and elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most appropriate diagnosis for this patient?

A 55-year-old woman comes to the clinic complaining of pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists for the past 6 weeks. She notes that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning, lasting for approximately 1 hour before improving as the day progresses. She has a history of hypothyroidism and hyperlipidemia, for which she takes levothyroxine and atorvastatin, respectively. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal: Hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL, Leukocyte count 9,800/mm3, Platelet count 350,000/mm3, Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 40 mm/h, Rheumatoid factor positive, Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-month history of joint swelling and stiffness. She reports that her symptoms are particularly severe in the morning, lasting more than an hour. She has noticed swelling and tenderness in her hands and wrists, making daily activities challenging. She denies any recent infections, fever, or weight loss. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. Her mother had rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, there is symmetric swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 38-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of chronic fatigue and muscle weakness over the past 6 months. She reports difficulty in concentrating and a noticeable decrease in her energy levels, which have impacted her daily activities. In addition, she has experienced increased sensitivity to cold, a recent weight gain of 5 kg (11 lbs), and dry skin. She denies any joint pain but mentions occasional muscle cramps. Her family history is significant for autoimmune disorders, with her mother having rheumatoid arthritis. Physical examination reveals dry, coarse skin and mild periorbital puffiness. Her temperature is 36.5°C (97.7°F), blood pressure is 115/70 mmHg, pulse is 58/min, and respirations are 16/min. Laboratory results show:
Hemoglobin 12 g/dL
Leukocytes 6,000/mm3
Platelets 250,000/mm3
TSH 15 µU/mL (normal: 0.5–5.0 µU/mL)
Free T4 0.6 ng/dL (normal: 0.8–1.8 ng/dL)
Anti-thyroid peroxidase antibody Positive
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 3-month history of shoulder pain. She describes the pain as a dull ache that is aggravated by overhead activities and lifting. She has difficulty combing her hair and reaching behind her back. Her past medical history is significant for type 2 diabetes mellitus. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the anterior shoulder and a reduced range of motion. There is no swelling or warmth. Neurological examination is normal. An X-ray of the shoulder shows no bony abnormalities. Laboratory tests show a normal white blood cell count, normal inflammatory markers, and a negative rheumatoid factor. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of joint pain and swelling in her hands and feet for the past 6 weeks. She notes that the pain is worse in the morning and improves as the day progresses. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension and she is a lifelong non-smoker. On physical examination, there is symmetric swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands, with tenderness to palpation. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Serological studies show positivity for anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to persistent pain in her hands and wrists for the past year. She describes the pain as worse in the morning and improving with activity throughout the day. She also notes the development of lumps under her skin around her elbows. Her medical history is notable for hypertension managed with lisinopril. She takes ibuprofen occasionally for pain, but it provides limited relief. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and wrist joints bilaterally, along with several subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of her arms. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated C-reactive protein. Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of fatigue, joint pain, and generalized weakness. She reports feeling exhausted despite adequate sleep and has noticed occasional bruising without significant trauma. Her past medical history is unremarkable. On examination, her temperature is 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure is 110/70 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. Laboratory studies reveal pancytopenia with hemoglobin of 9 g/dL, white blood cell count of 2,000/mm³, and platelet count of 90,000/mm³. The patient's ANA is positive, and ESR is elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of persistent dry eyes and dry mouth for the last 8 months. She has also noticed swelling in the region of her cheeks, particularly when eating. Her past medical history is significant for fatigue and occasional joint pain in her hands and knees, for which she takes ibuprofen as needed. On physical examination, there is bilateral enlargement of the parotid glands and dental caries. Laboratory tests reveal positive anti-SSA/Ro antibodies and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. What is the most appropriate next step in the diagnosis of this patient's condition?

options.F: Start artificial tears and saliva substitutes

question: A 36-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of progressive difficulty walking due to weakness in her lower extremities. She mentions feeling more fatigued than usual and has noticed some pain in her hips and knees, particularly in the morning. She denies any numbness or tingling in her legs. Her medical history is significant for mild hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. On examination, she has bilateral lower extremity weakness, more pronounced in the hip flexors. Reflexes are brisk but symmetrical. Sensation is intact. Laboratory studies reveal a mildly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and normal thyroid function tests. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of recurrent painful oral ulcers and genital sores. She also reports fatigue and occasional joint pain, particularly in the knees and ankles. She denies any fever, weight loss, or gastrointestinal symptoms. On examination, her vital signs are normal, and there are multiple aphthous ulcers noted in the oral cavity. Genital examination reveals ulcerative lesions on the labia. There is mild tenderness on palpation of the knees but no swelling or warmth. Laboratory tests show mild anemia and elevated C-reactive protein. What is the most likely diagnosis based on this clinical presentation?

A 62-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of persistent bone pain over the past 6 months. She describes the pain as dull and aching, primarily located in her hips and lower back. She has no history of trauma. The pain worsens with activity and improves somewhat with rest. She has been postmenopausal for 12 years and does not smoke or consume alcohol. Her medical history is significant for hypertension controlled with lisinopril. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the pelvis and decreased range of motion in the hips. Laboratory studies show:
Calcium 9.2 mg/dL
Phosphate 3.4 mg/dL
Alkaline phosphatase 420 U/L
25-hydroxyvitamin D 30 ng/mL
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of fatigue and joint pain. She notes the pain is bilateral and affects her wrists, hands, and knees. It is particularly severe upon waking and gradually improves over the course of the day. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies reveal hemoglobin of 11.0 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume of 89 fL, and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 45 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 2-year history of progressively worsening pain and swelling in her hands and wrists. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves with activity throughout the day. She has noted some deformity of her fingers and struggles with daily tasks such as opening jars. Physical examination reveals ulnar deviation and swan-neck deformities of the fingers. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive rheumatoid factor. Anti-citrullinated protein antibodies are also positive. X-rays of her hands reveal joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following findings is most closely associated with this patient's condition?

A 47-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of progressive joint pain and swelling. The symptoms primarily affect her wrists, metacarpophalangeal joints, and knees, and are worse in the morning, improving as the day progresses. She reports significant morning stiffness lasting over an hour. Her past medical history is unremarkable except for a family history of autoimmune disease. On examination, there is synovitis and tenderness in multiple joints with reduced grip strength. Laboratory studies show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor (RF) and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are both positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of progressive joint pain and deformity over the last 6 months. She notes that her symptoms are worse in the morning, often taking over an hour to improve. She has difficulty opening jars and notices her fingers look swollen and deformed. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, controlled on levothyroxine. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with notable ulnar deviation of the fingers. Laboratory tests reveal elevated rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 38-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of joint swelling and morning stiffness lasting over an hour for the past three months. She reports that her symptoms started in the small joints of her hands and gradually involved her wrists and ankles. She denies any fever, rash, or gastrointestinal symptoms. Her sister has rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Her laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) are elevated. A hand X-ray shows joint space narrowing and periarticular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of fatigue and joint pain. She reports stiffness in her fingers and wrists, especially in the morning, lasting over an hour. She also notes swelling in her knees and pain in her ankles. She has a family history of autoimmune diseases, including lupus in her mother. On physical examination, there is bilateral swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 10.5 g/dL
Mean corpuscular volume (MCV) 85 fL
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) 55 mm/h
Rheumatoid factor is positive. Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are elevated. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 38-year-old woman presents to her primary care clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain in both hands and wrists for the past 6 months. She describes the pain as worse in the morning, lasting for about an hour, and affecting both sides equally. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, managed with levothyroxine. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal hemoglobin of 11.2 g/dL, hematocrit of 34%, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr. A complete blood count shows normocytic anemia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of unexplained weight gain, periorbital edema, and joint stiffness, particularly in the morning. She reports feeling fatigued but denies any fever or night sweats. She has noticed her rings fitting more tightly than usual and joint pain in her fingers and wrists, but there is no significant redness or warmth. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she is not on any medications. Her vital signs are stable, with a temperature of 98.7°F (37.1°C), blood pressure of 115/75 mmHg, and a pulse of 68/min. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman is evaluated for worsening joint pain and swelling over the past 3 years. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour. She has had difficulty grasping objects and notices deformities in her fingers. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. She denies recent infections or trauma. On examination, there are ulnar deviations and swan-neck deformities of the fingers. Tenderness and swelling are noted at the wrists and small joints of the hands. Laboratory tests reveal positive rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies. X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of swelling and pain in her wrists, knees, and small joints of her hands for the past few weeks. She reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning and tends to improve as the day progresses. Her past medical history is unremarkable except for occasional headaches treated with acetaminophen. She denies recent trauma, fevers, or weight loss. Physical examination reveals boggy swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and a positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibody test. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 10-month history of bilateral hand and wrist pain, which she describes as an aching sensation. The pain is associated with morning stiffness lasting over an hour and improves throughout the day with activity. She also reports the presence of firm, nontender nodules on her elbows. Her past medical history is notable for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, as well as subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 60 mm/hr and positive rheumatoid factor. Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are also positive. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old female presents to her primary care physician with complaints of progressive weight gain over the past 8 months, alongside increasing periorbital edema and joint stiffness that is worse in the morning. She notes fatigue and a general feeling of malaise but denies significant joint pain. Her past medical history includes hypertension controlled with lisinopril and a recent diagnosis of type 2 diabetes, for which she is on metformin. Physical examination reveals puffiness around the eyes, mild pedal edema, and limited range of motion in the fingers, particularly in the morning. Laboratory tests show serum sodium of 140 mEq/L, serum creatinine of 1.0 mg/dL, and a urinary protein excretion of 4.5 g/day. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of her symptoms?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling in her fingers and wrists for the past six months. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour. Her medical history is notable for hypothyroidism, managed with levothyroxine. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). An X-ray of the hands shows periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to swelling and pain in multiple joints for the past 3 months. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves with activity. Her medical history is notable for type 2 diabetes mellitus, controlled with metformin. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints and wrists bilaterally. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein level. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are both positive. An X-ray of the hands shows periarticular osteopenia and mild joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of a 2-month history of joint swelling and morning stiffness lasting more than 1 hour. She reports swelling in her fingers and wrists, and difficulty performing daily tasks. She also notes fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers. Her medical history is significant for seasonal allergies, and she takes no medications regularly. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein level. Testing reveals positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 47-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of left shoulder pain and stiffness that has progressively worsened over the past 3 months. She denies any recent trauma or injury to the shoulder. The pain is constant and she notes difficulty reaching overhead and behind her back. Her past medical history includes Type 2 diabetes mellitus, well-controlled with metformin. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the anterolateral aspect of the shoulder and a significant restriction in both active and passive range of motion. There is no swelling, erythema, or warmth in the shoulder joint. Her laboratory tests show normal inflammatory markers, and a recent shoulder X-ray was unremarkable. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 28-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 2-week history of purpuric rash on her lower extremities and intermittent joint pain in her knees and ankles. She also reports noticing blood in her urine over the last several days. She denies any fevers, weight loss, or recent infections. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she is currently taking no medications. Physical examination reveals palpable purpura on the lower legs and mild swelling of the knees and ankles. Laboratory studies show a normal complete blood count and basic metabolic panel, but urinalysis reveals hematuria and mild proteinuria. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 48-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of progressively worsening joint pain and stiffness over the past two years. She reports morning stiffness lasting longer than an hour and difficulty with activities of daily living, such as buttoning her clothes. On examination, there is notable swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with ulnar deviation of fingers. Laboratory testing reveals a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. An X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 36-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling in both hands and wrists for the past 2 months. She describes morning stiffness lasting more than an hour, which improves as the day progresses. She also reports feeling fatigued and having low-grade fevers occasionally. Her past medical history includes well-controlled hypothyroidism. Family history reveals that her mother had an autoimmune disease, though she is unsure of the specifics. Physical examination shows swelling and tenderness of the proximal interphalangeal and metacarpophalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Her rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (ACPAs) are also present. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 33-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 6-month history of widespread musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, and disturbed sleep. She describes the pain as a constant ache, particularly in her neck, shoulders, and back. The fatigue has been severe enough to interfere with her daily activities and she often wakes up feeling unrefreshed despite 8 hours of sleep. She denies any significant morning stiffness or joint swelling. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. Physical examination reveals diffuse tenderness at multiple soft tissue points but no joint synovitis or deformities. Laboratory tests show normal complete blood count, normal C-reactive protein, and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most appropriate diagnosis?

A 56-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of chronic pain and stiffness in both hands and wrists for the past 6 months. She also notes the presence of firm nodules under the skin on her forearms. The pain is worse in the mornings and improves throughout the day. She denies fever, weight loss, or recent infections. Her past medical history is significant for well-controlled hypertension. She takes lisinopril daily and denies any allergies.

On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally with visible subcutaneous nodules on her forearms. Laboratory results show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) are elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of tingling and numbness in her feet and hands. She describes the sensation as 'pins and needles' and notes it is worse at night. In addition, she has experienced occasional joint pain in her fingers and wrists, but denies any swelling or morning stiffness. She has a past medical history of hypertension and type 2 diabetes, which is poorly controlled with a current HbA1c of 9.2%. Her current medications include metformin and lisinopril.

On examination, she has diminished sensation to light touch and vibration in a glove and stocking distribution. Muscle strength is normal throughout, and there is no joint erythema or deformity. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory results reveal a normal complete blood count, normal renal function, and negative antinuclear antibodies.

Which of the following is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

A 36-year-old woman visits her primary care provider due to pain and swelling in her hands and wrists for the past three months. She describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour and progressive difficulty performing daily activities due to joint discomfort. She has no significant past medical history and denies any recent trauma or infections. Her family history is notable for her mother having rheumatoid arthritis. On physical examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr (reference range: 0-20 mm/hr) and a C-reactive protein (CRP) level of 12 mg/L (reference range: 0-5 mg/L). Her rheumatoid factor is elevated at 70 IU/mL (reference range: 0-20 IU/mL), and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 40-year-old female presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of fatigue and joint pain. She reports stiffness in her hands and wrists that is worst in the morning and lasts for several hours. She denies any recent infections or travel. Her medical history is significant for mild hypertension managed with lisinopril. On physical examination, her vital signs are stable, and there is swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory tests reveal a normocytic anemia with a hemoglobin level of 10.5 g/dL (N: 12.0-15.5 g/dL) and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 68 mm/hr (N: 0-20 mm/hr). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of progressively worsening fatigue and joint pain. She describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour, affecting her wrists, knees, and small joints of both hands. She also reports generalized weakness and difficulty performing daily activities due to joint swelling. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she denies any recent infections or significant weight changes. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal normocytic anemia and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old female presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of left knee pain and swelling that began after a long hike two days ago. She describes the pain as sharp and reports difficulty bearing weight on the affected leg. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril, and osteoarthritis of the hands. She denies any history of trauma to the knee. On examination, she is afebrile with stable vital signs. The left knee is swollen, warm, and tender to touch with a decreased range of motion. There is no rash or systemic symptoms. Laboratory studies reveal a white blood cell count of 9,200/mm³ and a C-reactive protein level of 4 mg/L. Synovial fluid analysis from the knee shows elevated white blood cells and crystals. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman visits the clinic due to painful oral ulcers recurring every few months over the past year. She also reports genital sores that appear simultaneously, which are intensely painful and heal with scarring. Over the last two weeks, she has experienced joint pain in her knees and ankles. She denies fever or weight loss. Her medical history is unremarkable. Physical examination reveals multiple shallow ulcers on the buccal mucosa and similar lesions on the labia minora. There is mild erythema and swelling in the knee joints, but no effusion. Her vital signs are stable. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of persistent joint pain in her hands and wrists for the past 4 months. She describes morning stiffness lasting about 2 hours, and the pain improves as the day progresses. Her mother has a history of rheumatoid arthritis. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-year history of chronic pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists, which she notices are worse in the morning and improve throughout the day. She reports swelling of her fingers and difficulty making a fist. She also mentions the presence of firm lumps on her elbows. She has no significant past medical history and takes ibuprofen as needed for pain. Her family history is significant for her mother having an autoimmune disease, though she is unsure of the specifics. Physical examination reveals synovitis of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints with subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of the elbows. Laboratory findings show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 6-month history of joint pain and stiffness, primarily affecting her hands and wrists. She reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning and lasts for about an hour before improving with movement. She denies any history of trauma but does mention that her mother had similar symptoms and was diagnosed with a 'joint condition.' On examination, her metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints are swollen and tender bilaterally, with slight ulnar deviation of the fingers. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive rheumatoid factor. Radiographs of her hands show periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 68-year-old woman with a history of osteoporosis presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of severe lower back pain that started while she was bending over to pick up a laundry basket. She denies any recent trauma or fall. She describes the pain as sharp and radiating around her waistline. She has been experiencing generalized fatigue and mild joint stiffness in the morning for the past few months, which she attributed to aging. Her medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, for which she takes lisinopril and atorvastatin. On examination, there is localized tenderness over the lumbar spine, and she has difficulty standing upright due to pain. Neurological examination is normal. An X-ray of the lumbar spine demonstrates a wedge compression fracture of L2. Which of the following is most likely associated with this patient's presentation?

A 47-year-old woman presents to her primary care clinic with complaints of progressive weight gain over the past six months. She notes swelling around her eyes and generalized puffiness, particularly in the morning. Additionally, she reports experiencing joint stiffness in her wrists and ankles that improves as the day progresses. She has no significant medical history and denies any recent changes in diet or activity levels. Her medications include a daily multivitamin. Physical examination reveals periorbital edema and mild pitting edema of the lower extremities. Her blood pressure is 130/85 mm Hg, and her BMI is 31 kg/m2. Laboratory tests show:

Serum:
Na+: 138 mEq/L
K+: 4.1 mEq/L
HCO3-: 24 mEq/L
BUN: 16 mg/dL
Creatinine: 0.9 mg/dL
Albumin: 2.8 g/dL
AST: 22 U/L
ALT: 18 U/L

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 48-year-old woman is evaluated in the clinic due to recurrent episodes of pain and swelling in her right great toe over the past year. She reports that the episodes occur every few months and are associated with redness and tenderness, resolving spontaneously over weeks. She denies fever or systemic symptoms. Physical examination reveals mild swelling and erythema of the right first metatarsophalangeal joint, with a firm nodular lesion noted over the joint. Laboratory tests show normal complete blood count, serum creatinine, and liver function tests. Serum uric acid level is elevated. Radiographs of the foot show erosions of the first metatarsophalangeal joint. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and stiffness that she has been experiencing for the past 6 months. The stiffness is most pronounced in the mornings, lasting for about an hour, and affects her ability to perform daily activities, such as gripping objects or typing on her computer. She has noticed swelling in her wrists and bilateral metacarpophalangeal joints. She denies any recent trauma or fever. Her past medical history is significant for asthma, for which she uses an albuterol inhaler as needed. On examination, she has tenderness and swelling in her wrists and the proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory results show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's condition?

A 42-year-old woman visits the clinic with complaints of joint pain and stiffness in her hands and feet for the past 6 months. She describes the stiffness as more severe in the morning, lasting over an hour, and improving with activity. Her medical history is significant for a positive family history of autoimmune diseases. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal joints, proximal interphalangeal joints, and metatarsophalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and a positive anti-citrullinated protein antibody test. Radiographs of her hands demonstrate joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-month history of progressive difficulty walking. She reports weakness in her legs, worse in the evenings, and occasional falls. She also notes occasional joint pain in her wrists and knees. Her past medical history is significant for diabetes mellitus controlled with metformin. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. On physical exam, her vital signs are stable. Muscle strength in the lower extremities is 3/5 bilaterally. Deep tendon reflexes are diminished in the lower extremities. Sensation to light touch and pinprick is intact. Laboratory studies reveal elevated creatine phosphokinase (CPK) levels. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

A 58-year-old woman presents to the clinic with bilateral hip pain that has been progressively worsening over the past four months. She reports the pain is more pronounced in the morning and gradually improves throughout the day. She has noticed some difficulty in standing up from a seated position and climbing stairs due to the pain. The patient denies any history of trauma and has no significant past medical history. She is not taking any medications and denies alcohol or tobacco use. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the greater trochanter bilaterally, and her range of motion in the hips is somewhat limited. Laboratory tests reveal a mildly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and normal rheumatoid factor levels. X-rays of the hips show no fracture but indicate some narrowing of the joint space. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.D: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head

question: A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and stiffness. She reports that the stiffness is particularly severe in the morning, lasting for more than an hour, and affects her wrists, fingers, and knees symmetrically. She experiences fatigue and has noticed difficulty performing daily activities due to pain. She denies any recent trauma or change in medication. Her vital signs are normal. On physical examination, there is synovial thickening and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Mild ulnar deviation of the fingers is noted. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody. An X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 38-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of chronic neck pain and episodes of tingling and numbness in both arms. She reports that the neck pain often gets worse after prolonged periods of sitting at her desk, but improves slightly with rest and over-the-counter pain medication. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or significant weight loss. Physical examination reveals reduced range of motion in the cervical spine and decreased sensation to light touch over the lateral aspects of both forearms. Reflexes in the upper extremities are normal. MRI of the cervical spine shows disc protrusion at the C5-C6 level with mild neural foraminal narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with recurrent episodes of severe pain in her right big toe over the past 6 months. She describes the pain as intense and throbbing, often waking her from sleep, and it typically lasts for several days before gradually subsiding. She reports that the toe is red, swollen, and extremely tender during these episodes. Her past medical history includes obesity and hypertension, for which she is taking lisinopril. She has no known allergies and drinks alcohol occasionally. Physical examination reveals a swollen, erythematous right first metatarsophalangeal joint with a chalky white deposit overlying the joint. Serum uric acid levels are elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 44-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of fatigue, night sweats, and unintentional weight loss. She also reports intermittent low-grade fevers and joint pain affecting her wrists and knees. She denies any recent infections or travel. On physical examination, she has palpable cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy. Laboratory tests reveal normocytic anemia and elevated ESR. A chest X-ray shows mediastinal lymphadenopathy. A lymph node biopsy is performed and shows non-caseating granulomas. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of swelling and pain in her hands and wrists that has worsened over the last three months. She describes the pain as being most severe in the morning, lasting about 1 hour before improving as she starts her day. She also reports fatigue and a low-grade fever. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she denies recent infections or travel. On examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Serum testing reveals positive rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 53-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of pain and swelling in her hands and wrists. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty gripping objects. She denies any recent infections or trauma. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. On physical examination, she has symmetric swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to a 6-month history of widespread musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, and sleep disturbance. She reports that her symptoms interfere with her daily activities and have led to decreased work productivity. She also experiences stiffness in the morning that lasts for about 30 minutes. Physical examination shows multiple tender points but no swelling or erythema of the joints. Laboratory studies show normal erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-month history of fatigue, low-grade fevers, and firm, painless nodules under the skin of her forearms. She also reports occasional joint pain in her wrists and knees. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, controlled with levothyroxine. She works as a school teacher and denies any history of trauma, recent travel, or known allergies. Physical examination reveals multiple subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of her arms and elbows.
